17.6: The fall of Samaria (722 BCE) to the Assyrian Empire (under Sargon II, 2 Kgs 17:6) ended the northern kingdom of Israel after 210 years and 19 kings (every one of whom 'did evil in the sight of YHWH'). The author of Kings provides the theological explanation in 17:7-23: because Israel sinned against YHWH, walked in the customs of the nations, built high places, served Baal — YHWH removed them out of his sight. The Assyrian exile of the northern tribes ('the ten lost tribes') was permanent: they were never restored as a distinct entity. The northern exile is the first fulfillment of the Deuteronomic covenant curses (Deut 28:36, 64); the Babylonian exile of Judah (586 BCE) is the second. Both are explained as covenant consequences, not historical accidents.
17.1: Hoshea son of Elah begins his reign in Samaria in the 12th year of Ahaz (c. 732 BCE), reigning nine years (c. 732–723 BCE). He is the last king of the northern kingdom — 19 kings from Jeroboam I to Hoshea, none of whom turned from the sins of Jeroboam. Hoshea's name (hôšēaʿ — salvation, deliverance) is the same root as Joshua and Jesus; the final northern king bears the name of salvation but delivers his kingdom into captivity. He was installed by Tiglath-pileser III after killing Pekah (15:30) and was from the beginning an Assyrian vassal.
17.2: The evaluative formula for Hoshea is uniquely qualified: 'He did what was evil in the sight of the LORD, though not to the degree of the kings of Israel before him' (rāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH raq lōʾ ḵᵉmalkê Yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer hāyû lᵉpānāyw). This is the only northern king to receive a qualified negative evaluation. The basis of the qualification is unclear — possibly Hoshea permitted more direct YHWH worship, possibly the Deuteronomist is acknowledging that even within the unbroken negative trajectory there were degrees of apostasy. The qualification does not save Hoshea or the kingdom; the trajectory toward exile is unaffected by relative improvement.
17.3: Shalmaneser V (ruled 727–722 BCE) — son and successor of Tiglath-pileser III — marches against Hoshea. Hoshea 'became his vassal and paid him tribute' (wayhî lô ʿeḇed wayyāšeḇ lô minḥāh); the minḥāh (tribute, literally gift-offering) is the same word used for the sacrificial grain offering, suggesting the political tribute-language and the cultic offering-language share a vocabulary of submission. Hoshea's vassalage is initially compliant, continuing the tribute-payment pattern established by Menahem.
17.4: Hoshea's conspiracy with 'So king of Egypt' (śōʾ melek miṣrāyim) and withholding of annual tribute triggers Shalmaneser's response. 'So' has been variously identified as Osorkon IV (the last pharaoh of the 22nd dynasty, ruling in Tanis), Tefnakht of Sais (a Delta king), or Sibe the Egyptian commander mentioned in Sargon II's annals. Egypt was entering a period of political fragmentation (the Third Intermediate Period) and was not capable of providing effective military assistance; the appeal to Egypt was strategically futile. Hoshea's arrest and imprisonment before the full siege indicates Shalmaneser may have intercepted the diplomatic correspondence, exposing the conspiracy before Hoshea could fully mobilize against Assyria.
17.5: The three-year siege of Samaria (c. 725–722 BCE) is one of the longer sieges recorded in the biblical narrative. Samaria, built on a naturally defensible hill (purchased by Omri, 1 Kgs 16:24), was difficult to take by direct assault; Assyrian siege techniques (ramps, battering rams, siege towers) required time against a well-supplied hilltop city. The three-year resistance indicates significant food and water reserves in the city. Shalmaneser V died during or shortly after the siege; his successor Sargon II claims the actual capture in his own annals (ANET 284-285: 'I besieged and conquered Samaria, led away as booty 27,290 inhabitants of it').
17.7: The Deuteronomist begins the longest theological explanation in Kings (vv7-23), unparalleled in scope in the entire narrative. The explanation opens with the covenantal framework: 'This happened because the Israelites had sinned against the LORD their God, who had brought them out of Egypt' (wayhî kî ḥāṭᵉʾû bᵉnê-Yiśrāʾēl laYHWH ʾelōhêhem hammaʿᵃleh ʾōtām mēʾereṣ miṣrāyim). The Exodus is the defining covenant act; Israel's election and obligation is rooted in YHWH's redemptive initiative. Sin is framed as covenant violation — not merely moral failure but the betrayal of a specific relationship established at Sinai. Worshipping other gods is the fundamental breach (v7b: wayyirᵉʾû ʾelōhîm ʾăḥērîm).
17.8: The charge that Israel 'followed the practices of the nations the LORD had driven out before them' (wayyēlᵉḵû bᵉḥuqqôt haggôyim ʾăšer hôrîš YHWH mippᵉnê bᵉnê Yiśrāʾēl) is the Deuteronomistic irony: Israel was to be the replacement population for the nations; instead, Israel adopted the nations' practices and is now itself replaced by imported nations (v24). The divine-expulsion logic that justified the Conquest (Deut 9:4-5 — the nations were driven out for their wickedness, not Israel's righteousness) now applies to Israel: YHWH expels covenant-breakers regardless of their ethnic identity.
17.9: Israel 'secretly did things against the LORD their God that were not right' (wayhappāsēq bᵉnê Yiśrāʾēl dᵉḇārîm ʾăšer lōʾ ḵēn ʿal YHWH ʾelōhêhem) — the word hippāsēq (Piel of a root meaning to cover, conceal) indicates the covert nature of the apostasy. The installation of high places 'from the smallest watchtower to the largest fortified city' (mimmigdal nōṣᵉrîm ʿaḏ ʿîr miḇṣār) describes comprehensive geographic coverage: every inhabited site, from the most remote watchpost to the most fortified urban center, had unauthorized cultic installations. The scale of the apostasy matches the scale of the judgment.
17.10: The sacred pillars (maṣṣēḇôt) and Asherah poles (ʾăšērîm) are the two most frequently condemned cult installations in the Deuteronomistic literature. Maṣṣēḇôt were standing stones used as markers of divine presence, originally acceptable in the patriarchal period (Gen 28:18) but condemned in Deut 12:3 and 16:22. ʾĂšērîm were wooden poles or trees representing Asherah, the Canaanite mother-goddess and consort of El; archaeological evidence from Kuntillet Ajrud (c. 800 BCE) includes inscriptions referencing 'YHWH of Samaria and his Asherah,' indicating that Asherah veneration was integrated into some forms of YHWH worship. The combination of sacred stones and Asherah poles on 'every high hill and under every green tree' is the standard Deuteronomistic description of full-spectrum Canaanite syncretism.
17.11: 'They burned offerings at all the high places as the nations had done' (wayhaqṭᵉrû šām bᵉḵol-habbāmôt kaggôyim ʾăšer heglāh YHWH mippᵉnêhem) returns to the governing irony: Israel's behavior mirrors the expelled nations. The phrase wayyaʿᵃśû dᵉḇārîm rāʿîm lᵉḵaʿᵃs ʾet-YHWH, 'they did evil things to provoke the LORD to anger,' introduces the kaʿas (provocation) theme that runs through the Deuteronomistic assessment of the kings; YHWH's response to kaʿas is the withdrawal of protection and the activation of covenant curses.
17.12: Israel 'served idols' (wayyaʿaḇdû haggillûlîm) — the word gillûlîm (used 38 times in Ezekiel and here) is a highly polemical term, often understood as a derivation meaning 'dung-pellets' or 'dung-balls,' a deliberate scatological coinage to disparage idols. The explicit divine prohibition — ʾăšer ʾāmar lahem YHWH lōʾ taʿᵃśûn ʾet-haddāḇār hazzeh, 'despite the LORD saying to them: do not do this thing' — frames the idolatry as willful defiance of a clear command rather than ignorance. The Deuteronomistic framework is that Israel had full covenantal knowledge (Deut 4:15-20 explicitly warns against the gods represented by the host of heaven and carved images) and violated it deliberately.
17.13: YHWH's prophetic warnings — sent 'through every prophet and seer' (bᵉyaḏ kol nāḇîʾ kol ḥōzeh) — form the second half of the theological indictment: not only was Israel warned by the written law, but YHWH sent continuous prophetic messengers. The specific content of the warning is given: šûḇû middarᵉḵêḵem hārāʿîm wᵉšimrû miṣwōtay ḥuqqôtay kᵉḵol-hattôrāh ʾăšer ṣiwwîtî ʾet ʾăḇōtêḵem wᵉkāʾăšer šālaḥtî ʾalêḵem bᵉyaḏ ʿᵃḇāḏay hanᵉḇîʾîm, 'turn from your evil ways and keep my commandments and statutes — all the law I gave your ancestors, which I sent to you through my servant the prophets.' The prophets are the living voice of the written Torah; their cumulative rejection across 200 years of the northern kingdom is the measure of the apostasy's depth.
17.14: Israel 'would not listen but hardened their neck like their ancestors who had not trusted in the LORD their God' (wᵉlōʾ šāmēʿû wayyaqšû ʾet ʿārpām kᵉʿōrep ʾăḇōtām ʾăšer lōʾ heʾěmînû baYHWH ʾelōhêhem). The 'stiff neck' image (qᵉšēh ʿōrep) is first used in Exod 32:9 to describe Israel at the golden-calf incident; the Deuteronomist draws an explicit line from the wilderness apostasy to the northern kingdom's exile, framing the entire history as a single continuous failure. The contrast with faith (heʾěmîn — trusted, believed) is significant: the prophets called for covenant trust; Israel chose autonomy.
17.15: Israel 'rejected his statutes and the covenant he had made with their ancestors, and the warnings he had given them; they pursued worthless idols and became worthless themselves' (wayyimᵉʾăsû ʾet ḥuqqāyw wᵉʾet bᵉrîtô ʾăšer kārat ʾet ʾăḇôtām wᵉʾēt ʿēḏwōtāyw ʾăšer hēʿîḏ bām wayyēlᵉḵû ʾaḥᵃrê hahaḇel wayyehbālû). The wordplay on heḇel (vanity, vapor, worthlessness) — 'they pursued hebel and became hebel' — is one of Kings' most pointed theological formulations. Idols are heḇel (the word Ecclesiastes will use for all transient things; the name Abel/Hebel in Gen 4 may carry the same resonance of brevity). Israel became what it worshipped. The surrounding nations' practices that YHWH had forbidden to Israel are the standard of comparison: Israel's holiness was supposed to be the distinction; apostasy erased it.
17.16: The full apostasy catalogue: abandoned all YHWH's commandments → cast two metal calves (ʿēgālîm massēḵāh šᵉnayim, Jeroboam's calves of Bethel and Dan, 1 Kgs 12:28) → made an Asherah pole → worshipped the whole host of heaven (kol ṣᵉḇāʾ haššāmayim, astral worship including sun, moon, stars, and the divine council in astral form) → served Baal. The calf worship, Asherah, and astral worship are all attested in the Iron Age archaeological record from Israelite sites: Megiddo, Hazor, Beth-shan, and Tell el-Far'ah North all show evidence of non-YHWH cult objects alongside Israelite material culture. The catalogue is structured to show comprehensive abandonment: no commandment or covenant feature is left untouched.
17.17: The final items in the apostasy catalogue — burning children in the fire, practicing divination (qāsam qᵉsāmîm) and sorcery (nāḥaš niḥûšîm), and selling themselves completely to evil (wayyiḥtamkᵉrû lāʿăśôt hārāʿ) — represent the most severe Deuteronomic prohibitions. Child sacrifice is the capital offense of Lev 20:2; divination and sorcery are condemned in Deut 18:10-12 as the defining practices of the nations YHWH expelled; the phrase 'sold themselves to do evil' echoes 1 Kgs 21:20 (Ahab 'sold himself to do evil') — the voluntary, commercial metaphor for sin as self-enslavement. The comprehensive nature of the apostasy list justifies the comprehensive nature of the punishment.
17.18: 'So the LORD was extremely angry with Israel and expelled them from his sight' (wayyitʾannap YHWH mᵉʾōḏ bᵉYiśrāʾēl wayyᵉsîrēm mēʿal pānāyw lōʾ nišʾar raq šēḇeṭ yᵉhûḏāh lᵉḇaddô). The verb ʾānap (be angry) with the intensifier mᵉʾōḏ (exceedingly) marks the divine anger at its highest register in the narrative. 'Expelled from his sight' (wayyᵉsîrēm mēʿal pānāyw) is the ultimate covenant curse — the loss of divine presence, the reversal of the central covenant promise (Exod 33:14: 'My presence will go with you'). The note that 'only the tribe of Judah remained' is simultaneously a word of partial grace — the Davidic covenant still holds one tribe — and an anticipation of ch 25, where Judah will follow the same trajectory to the same judgment 136 years later.
17.19: The addendum — 'Judah also did not obey the commands of the LORD their God but followed the practices that Israel had introduced' (gam Yᵉhûḏāh lōʾ šāmar ʾet miṣwōt YHWH ʾelōhêhem wayyēlᵉḵû bᵉḥuqqôt Yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer ʿāśû) — is a theological footnote that prevents the reader from taking comfort in Judah's survival. Israel pioneered the apostasy; Judah followed. The exile of the north is not just Israel's story but a warning about Judah's trajectory. The Kings account will trace Judah's subsequent spiral through Manasseh's extreme apostasy (ch 21) to Josiah's reform (ch 22-23) to the final Babylonian deportations (ch 24-25).
17.20: 'The LORD rejected all the descendants of Israel, afflicted them, and handed them over to raiders, until he had expelled them from his sight' (wayyimʾas YHWH bᵉḵol zeraʿ Yiśrāʾēl wayyᵉʿannēm wayyittᵉnēm bᵉyaḏ šōsîm ʿaḏ ʾăšer hišlîḵām mēʿal pānāyw). The verb māʾas (reject, spurn) is used by Hosea (Hos 4:6: 'since you have rejected knowledge, I will reject you from being my priest') and Amos (Amos 2:4: 'because they rejected the law of the LORD') — the prophetic interpretation and the Deuteronomistic explanation use the same vocabulary. The 'raiders' who afflicted Israel during the period of the judges through the kings are reframed as YHWH's instruments of progressive punishment, building toward the final expulsion.
17.21: The narrative traces the apostasy back to its origin point: 'when Israel was torn away from the house of David, they made Jeroboam son of Nebat king, and Jeroboam led Israel away from following the LORD and caused them to commit great sin' (kî qāraʿ Yiśrāʾēl mēʿal bêt Dāwîḏ wayyamlîḵû ʾet Yarāḇᵉʿām ben-Nᵉḇāṭ... wayyaddaḥ Yarāḇᵉʿām ʾet Yiśrāʾēl mēʾaḥᵃrê YHWH waheḥĕṭîʾām ḥaṭṭāʾāh gᵉḏôlāh). The Schism (1 Kgs 12) is identified as the moment when the trajectory toward exile became inevitable: not because it was geopolitically destabilizing, but because Jeroboam's substitute cult (the golden calves) permanently redirected northern worship away from Jerusalem and toward an idolatrous alternative. The 210 years from Jeroboam to Hoshea are a single long act of national apostasy rooted in one founding sin.
17.22: 'The Israelites continued in all the sins Jeroboam had committed; they never turned away from them' (wayyēlᵉḵû bᵉnê Yiśrāʾēl bᵉḵol ḥaṭṭāʾôt Yarāḇᵉʿām ʾăšer ʿāśāh lōʾ sārû mimmennāh) — the climax of the theological explanation's backward reach. The entire history of the northern kings, with their individual variations in wickedness (some worse than others, but none departing from Jeroboam's core sin), is summarized in a single unbroken trajectory. The phrase lōʾ sārû mimmennāh (they did not turn from it) is the negative of the repentance (šûḇ) the prophets consistently called for.
17.23: 'Until the LORD removed Israel from his sight, just as he had declared through all his servants the prophets. So Israel was carried into exile from their land to Assyria — where they remain to this day' (ʿad ʾăšer-hēsîr YHWH ʾet Yiśrāʾēl mēʿal pānāyw kaʾăšer dibber bᵉyaḏ kol ʿăḇāḏāyw hanᵉḇîʾîm wayyigl Yiśrāʾēl mēʿal ʾadmātô ʾaššûr ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh). The phrase 'to this day' (ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh) is a historiographic marker indicating the exile was recent and living memory for the text's audience. The Deuteronomistic History was compiled/finalized during or shortly after the Babylonian exile; the northern exile had been a reality for over a century. 'As he declared through all his servants the prophets' ties the historical event to the prophetic corpus — the exile validates the prophets rather than refuting them.
17.24: The Assyrian resettlement policy — bringing populations from Babylon, Cuthah, Avva, Hamath, and Sepharvaim to replace the deported Israelites — is part of Sargon II's documented imperial strategy of population interchange. Sargon's annals state: 'the people of the lands conquered by my hands... I settled there; Assyrian administrators I set over them.' The imported populations came from multiple Mesopotamian and Syrian regions, creating a mixed society in the former northern kingdom. The later Samaritan identity grows from this mixed population — the returning exiles under Ezra and Nehemiah viewed Samaritans as disqualified from temple participation (Ezra 4:1-3) precisely because their ancestry was not purely Israelite. The NT Samaritan context (John 4, Luke 10:25-37) inherits this historical and ethnic complexity.
17.25: The settler populations' ignorance of 'the requirements of the god of the land' (mišpaṭ ʾelōhê hāʾāreṣ) triggers a lion attack, which YHWH sends. The ANE background is territorial-deity theology (see the Naaman narrative, 5:17): every land had its god, whose requirements had to be met to avoid divine punishment. The lions are a covenant curse from Lev 26:22 ('I will send wild beasts among you which shall rob you of your children and destroy your cattle and make you few in number') — though here applied to settlers rather than Israel. The dynamic is that even the pagan settlers recognize the principle of territorial divine management, while Israel (the people who should have known YHWH's requirements) failed to follow them.
17.26: The report to the Assyrian king — that the settler peoples don't know the mišpaṭ ʾelōhê hāʾāreṣ (requirements of the land's god) — uses precisely the territorial-deity framework that Naaman operated within (5:17). The irony is multilayered: non-Israelite settlers recognize that YHWH controls this land and that his requirements must be met; the Israelites who knew his covenant requirements never properly met them. The king of Assyria's practical response (send a priest to teach them) treats YHWH as one of many territorial deities to be managed — a thoroughly ANE-pagan interpretation of the God whose covenant with Israel Israel itself had treated with equal casualness.
17.27: The Assyrian king's command — send back one of the exiled priests to live in Bethel and teach the people 'the requirements of the god of the land' — is historically plausible as Assyrian administrative management: deportation policy was pragmatic, and a resident population required religious stability for political management. The specific instruction to send the priest to Bethel is pointed: Bethel was the site of Jeroboam's golden-calf sanctuary (1 Kgs 12:29), the epicenter of the northern kingdom's original apostasy. The priest who teaches YHWH worship there is inheriting a fundamentally compromised cult site.
17.28: The returning Israelite priest settles in Bethel and teaches the people 'how to worship the LORD' (wayyᵉhi mᵉlammēḏ ʾōtām ʾêḵ yîrᵉʾû ʾet YHWH). The verb yārēʾ (fear, revere) is the standard covenant-fear vocabulary; the priest presumably teaches some form of YHWH worship. However, the content of his teaching is compromised by two factors: (1) the Bethel location was associated with the calf-worship that the Deuteronomist considered fundamental apostasy; (2) the settlers continue their own deity practices alongside (vv29-33). The teaching produces syncretism, not conversion.
17.29: The imported nations manufacture their own gods — wayhî maʿᵃśēh kol gôy wᵉgôy ʾelōhêhem, 'each nation made its gods' — and install them in the high-place shrines (bêt habbāmôt) the Samaritans had built. The high-place infrastructure that the Israelites had built for syncretic YHWH worship is now repurposed for purely pagan worship by the immigrant populations. Each settler community imports its own national deity into the Israelite sacred geography, creating a religious patchwork that reverses the Conquest's purification of the land's cult sites.
17.30: The Babylonians made Succoth-benoth (Sukkôt bᵉnôt — possibly 'the booths of the daughters,' a name that may relate to a Babylonian astral deity, possibly Zarpanitum, consort of Marduk); the Cutheans made Nergal (the Babylonian underworld deity, god of pestilence and the dead, worshipped at Cutha/Tell Ibrahim in modern Iraq); the Hamathites made Ashima (ʾăšîmāʾ — a deity attested in Aramaic inscriptions from Hamath, possibly a form of Ishtar or a local deity). These deities are largely confirmed in cuneiform and Aramaic sources; the biblical naming preserves historical memory of the specific cult communities that settled in the former northern territory.
17.31: The Avvites made Nibhaz (niḇḥaz — possibly a dog-deity, corresponding to the Elamite deity Ibnahaza or a corruption of Anubis) and Tartak (unidentified; possibly an Aramaean deity). The Sepharvites burned their children to Adrammelech (ʾaḏrammeleḵ — possibly a form of the Mesopotamian deity Adad combined with melek, 'king') and Anammelech (ʿanammeleḵ — possibly Anu + melek, the Mesopotamian sky god). The child sacrifice practices of the Sepharvites mirror Ahaz's child sacrifice to Molech (16:3), creating an ironic parallel: the sin that characterized Judah's worst king is now practiced by pagan settlers in the former northern territory. The land that could not tolerate Israel's sins is now occupied by peoples practicing the same or worse sins.
17.32: The settlers 'professed to fear the LORD' (wayhû yᵉrēʾîm ʾet YHWH) while appointing their own priests for the high places. The appointment of non-Levitical, ethnically mixed priests for the high-place shrines parallels Jeroboam's appointment of non-Levitical priests (1 Kgs 12:31, 13:33); both represent the principle that the cult can be managed by whoever is politically convenient rather than whoever is covenantally designated. The fear vocabulary here is the language of pagan territorial-deity management, not the covenant-fear the law demands.
17.33: 'They worshiped the LORD, but they also served their own gods in the way of the nations from which they had come' (ʾet YHWH hāyû yᵉrēʾîm wᵉʾet ʾelōhêhem hāyû ʿōḇdîm kᵉmišpaṭ haggôyim ʾăšer heglû ʾōtām miššām). This is the defining description of Samaritan religion: syncretistic fear of YHWH combined with ethnic-deity worship. The phrase 'in the way of the nations' (kᵉmišpaṭ haggôyim) uses the same mišpaṭ (requirement, custom, legal standard) that the settlers initially lacked (v25-26): they now have a mišpaṭ for YHWH alongside their ethnic mišpaṭ. The result is a syncretistic religion indistinguishable from the Canaanite cult the Conquest was meant to remove.
17.34: 'To this day they follow their original practices. They do not truly fear the LORD or observe the statutes, ordinances, laws, and commandments he gave to the descendants of Jacob, whom he named Israel' (ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh hēm ʿōśîm kammišpāṭîm hārišōnîm ʾênām yᵉrēʾîm ʾet YHWH wᵉʾênām ʿōśîm kᵉḥuqqōtām ûkᵉmišpāṭêhem ûḵattôrāh ûḵammmiṣwāh ʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾet bᵉnê yaʿᵃqōḇ ʾăšer śām šᵉmô Yiśrāʾēl). The use of the name Israel — 'descendants of Jacob whom he named Israel' — asserts that the name Israel belongs properly to the covenant community descended from Jacob, not to the mixed settler population of Samaria. This naming-assertion anticipates the post-exilic controversy over who constitutes true Israel (Ezra 4, Neh 13).
17.35: YHWH's covenant command (vv35-39) is cited in full as the standard the settlers are failing to meet. The prohibition — 'you must not worship other gods or bow down to them or serve them or sacrifice to them' (lōʾ tîrᵉʾû ʾelōhîm ʾăḥērîm wᵉlōʾ tišᵉtaḥᵃwû lāhem wᵉlōʾ taʿᵃḇdûm wᵉlōʾ tizbᵉḥû lāhem) — restates the first and second commandments of the Decalogue (Exod 20:3-5). The fourfold prohibition (fear, bow down, serve, sacrifice) covers the full range of cultic acts; the Samaritan settlers' practices (vv32-33) violate all four. The covenant is cited not to condemn the settlers (they were not party to it) but to define what true fear of YHWH looks like, in contrast to their syncretistic practice.
17.36: 'Rather, fear the LORD who brought you out of Egypt with great power and an outstretched arm' (kî ʾim ʾet YHWH ʾăšer heʿᵃlāh ʾetḵem mēʾereṣ miṣrāyim bᵉḵōaḥ gāḏôl ûḇizrôaʿ nᵉṭûyāh ʾōtô tîrᵉʾû wᵉlô tišᵉtaḥᵃwû wᵉlô tizbᵉḥû). The Exodus formulation is the defining ground of Israelite covenant obligation: YHWH's election-act creates the exclusive covenant claim. The 'outstretched arm' (zᵉrôaʿ nᵉṭûyāh) is standard Exodus liturgical language (Exod 6:6, Deut 4:34, 5:15, 7:19). The settlers who had no part in the Exodus cannot respond to this appeal from within; the text's citation of it functions more as an indictment of Israel's failure to respond than as a command to the settlers.
17.37: The comprehensive command — observe forever the statutes, ordinances, laws, and commandments YHWH wrote for his people — uses four distinct legal terms: ḥuqqôt (statutes), mišpāṭîm (ordinances/judgments), tôrāh (law/instruction), miṣwāh (commandment). This legal quadruple also appears in 2 Chr 19:10 and reflects the Deuteronomistic understanding that the Torah has a comprehensive character: not just commandments or just statutes, but the entire integrated legal-covenantal deposit. The command to observe them 'forever' (kol hayyāmîm, all the days) excludes the possibility of situational non-compliance.
17.38: 'Do not forget the covenant I have made with you' (wᵉhabbᵉrît ʾăšer kāratî ʾittᵉḵem lōʾ tišᵉkāḥû) is the central anti-forgetting command of Deuteronomic theology. Deut 4:9, 6:12, and 8:11 repeatedly command Israel not to forget YHWH and his covenant; the prophets repeatedly charge Israel with forgetting (Hos 8:14, 13:6). The history of the northern kingdom is precisely the history of covenantal forgetting: the Torah was not read publicly, the Passover was not kept (until Josiah, 23:22), and the covenant obligations were treated as optional rather than constitutive of national identity.
17.39: 'Fear only the LORD your God, and he will rescue you from all your enemies' (kî ʾim ʾet YHWH ʾelōhêḵem tîrᵉʾû wᵉhûʾ yaṣṣîl ʾetḵem mikkol ʾōyᵉḇêḵem) is the covenant's positive promise: monolatrous fear of YHWH yields military protection. The irony is that Israel sought protection from Assyria through tribute (Menahem), through coalition politics (Pekah), and through Egyptian alliance (Hoshea) — every avenue except covenant fidelity. The verse anticipates Hezekiah's vindication (ch 18-19), where singular fear of YHWH (rather than Assyrian tribute or Egyptian alliance) results in miraculous deliverance from Sennacherib.
17.40: 'But they would not listen; they kept to their old ways' (wᵉlōʾ šāmēʿû kî ʾim kᵉmišpāṭām hārîʾšôn hēm ʿōśîm). The verse closes the covenant-exposition section with the same stubborn non-response that the text attributed to Israel in v14: wᵉlōʾ šāmēʿû (they did not listen). The settlers who replaced the Israelites exhibit the same fundamental orientation — following original practices rather than YHWH's covenant — that characterized the Israelites throughout the northern kingdom's history. The replacements are not an improvement; the land continues under the same spiritual pattern.
17.41: 'So these peoples worshiped the LORD but also served their own idols. Their children and grandchildren do the same today, just as their ancestors did' (wayhû haggôyim hāʾēlleh yᵉrēʾîm ʾet YHWH wᵉʾet pᵉsilêhem hāyû ʿōḇdîm gam bᵉnêhem ûḇnê bᵉnêhem kaʾăšer ʿāśû ʾăḇōtām hēm ʿōśîm ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh). The closing verse of ch 17 is simultaneously a historical statement about the Samaritan settlers and an anticipation of the Samaritan-Jewish controversy that runs from Ezra through the NT. The 'to this day' again connects the narrative to the author's present. The Samaritan woman at the well (John 4:20: 'Our fathers worshipped on this mountain, but you say Jerusalem is the place') and the healed Samaritan's gratitude (Luke 17:16) both operate against the background of this centuries-old religious ambiguity that v41 captures: part-worship of YHWH, part-worship of other gods, established as the inherited pattern of the land.

4.10: The Shunammite’s hospitality to Elisha — constructing a dedicated ʿaliyyat qîr (a walled upper room with a bed, table, chair, and lamp) — reflects both the ANE hospitality tradition for wandering holy men and the particular Israelite institution of the ʾîš ʾelōhîm (man of God) as a recognized social-religious figure supported by wealthy patrons. The ʿaliyyāh (upper room/chamber) was a distinct room typically built on the roof or as a second story, providing the most private and prestigious space in the house; the furnishing details (bed, table, chair, lamp) indicate a permanent guest room for regular use, not temporary hospitality. Comparable ANE parallels: the Ugaritic text KTU 1.17 depicts the hero Danel receiving a divine oracle after offering hospitality to the divine messengers, establishing the reciprocal pattern of hospitality-to-divine-agent yielding divine provision. The Elijah/Zarephath widow parallel (1 Kgs 17:19-23: Elijah brought to the widow’s upper room, raises her son there) establishes the ʿaliyyāh as the space for the greatest miracle: the son raised in the upper room of the man of God. Both ‘upper room’ episodes anticipate the NT’s upper room (Mark 14:15: anagaion) where the Last Supper is held, and Acts 1:13 where the disciples await the Spirit — the upper room as the space of covenant renewal and resurrection presence.
4.1: The woman identifies her husband as a member of the b’nê hannebî’îm, ‘sons of the prophets’ — a prophetic guild community associated with Elisha. These were communal groups (cf. 2 Kgs 6:1–7) who lived under the supervision of a prophetic leader; their members were not necessarily blood relatives of Elijah or Elisha but disciples or associates. The legal backdrop is Exod 21:7 (and Lev 25:39–41): Israelite debt-slavery law permitted a creditor to seize a debtor’s children as bond-servants when the debt could not be paid. The widow’s appeal to Elisha is a patronage appeal — she invokes her husband’s loyalty to the prophetic community as a claim on Elisha’s intercession.
4.2: Olive oil was a primary commodity in the ancient Near East — used for cooking, lighting (lamp fuel), cosmetics, medicinal purposes, and temple ritual. The widow’s single jar (ʾāsûḵ, a container for anointing oil) is a marginal supply, barely enough for personal use. Elisha’s instructions to gather empty vessels from neighbors engage the village economy: such borrowing was a recognized social practice in which community solidarity partially buffered individual economic collapse.
4.3: Elisha’s instruction to borrow ‘empty containers’ and ‘not too few’ (ʾal-tamʿîṭî) frames the miracle as contingent on the widow’s faith and initiative — the number of vessels gathered would determine the volume of oil produced. This structure — where the scope of divine provision tracks the human act of prepared receptivity — appears also in the widow of Zarephath account (1 Kgs 17) and in Gospel multiplication narratives. ANE parallel: the goddess Asherah was associated with oil abundance, but the text deliberately attributes the provision to YHWH through the prophetic word alone.
4.4: The instruction to ‘shut the door behind you and your sons’ withdraws the miracle from public view — a pattern in Elisha’s ministry (cf. 2 Kgs 4:33, where he also shuts the door in the Shunammite resurrection account). The closed-door setting emphasizes that the miracle is not a public spectacle or a demonstration of prophetic power but a private act of provision. The woman’s sons function as assistants; their presence links the miracle to the family unit that would otherwise be enslaved.
4.5: The narrative slows to emphasize repetition — ‘they kept bringing vessels while she kept pouring’ — using the Hebrew imperfect to convey continued action. The scene is entirely domestic: mother and sons working in an enclosed space with borrowed household equipment. The extraordinary emerges within the ordinary rhythms of village labor. Neighbors’ vessels represent the entire social network the family could access; when those are exhausted, the provision ceases.
4.6: The oil stops precisely when the last borrowed vessel is filled — not before, not after. This detail underlines the interpretive framework established by v3: the scale of provision matched the scope of preparation. The widow’s statement ‘there are no more vessels’ functions as the natural limiting condition; the miracle operates within the constraints of the human response. Comparable ANE royal inscriptions sometimes attribute agricultural abundance to divine favor, but those are idealized propaganda; this account is intimate and economically specific.
4.7: Elisha’s solution is practical: sell the oil, pay the debt, live on the surplus. The language of debt-payment (šallᵉmî ʾet-niššayēḵ) uses the same root (šālem) as shalom — debt cancellation as a form of restoration and wholeness. The surplus oil that remains after debt payment provides ongoing subsistence for the widow and her sons. The miracle does not make the family wealthy; it cancels their liability and re-establishes their social standing in the community.
4.8: Shunem (modern Sulam) is a village in the Jezreel Valley in Issachar, some 15–20 km from Carmel. A ‘great woman’ (ʾiššâ gᵉdôlāh) denotes high social standing — wealth, land-ownership, and community influence, not merely personal virtue. Her pressing Elisha to eat (using a verb of urgency, ḥāzaq) is hospitality initiative consistent with ANE aristocratic norms: receiving a known prophet was an act of honor and an invitation to divine favor. The Shunammite becomes a patron to the itinerant prophet.
4.9: The Shunammite’s declaration that Elisha is ‘a holy man of God’ (ʾîš ’ĕlōhîm qādôš hûʾ) uses the full honorific that marks prophetic office. The term qādôš (holy) is rare as a human descriptor in the Hebrew Bible — it signals that Elisha is set apart for divine service in a way that is perceptible to a discerning observer. The Shunammite’s theological discernment contrasts with the king’s and other officials’ tendency to miss the prophetic presence; she acts on what she perceives.
4.11: The guest room Elisha uses when passing through Shunem becomes the setting for the subsequent death-and-resurrection narrative (vv. 18–37). The narrative marker ‘one day he came there, went up to the room, and rested’ establishes the routine of his visits. The prophet’s reliance on the Shunammite’s hospitality inverts normal patron-client dynamics: she provides; he will ultimately provide the gift she did not ask for (a son) and cannot replace (the son’s life).
4.12: Elisha communicates through Gehazi his servant rather than directly with the Shunammite woman — a protocol detail that reflects ancient hierarchies. Speaking through an intermediary maintains social distance appropriate to a single male prophet and a married woman; Gehazi becomes the official channel of communication. The Shunammite’s social standing is evident: she ‘stood before him’ (wattaʿămod lᵉpānāyw) — the posture of a social equal presenting herself at a formal audience, not the posture of a supplicant.
4.13: Elisha’s offer to intercede with the king or the commander of the army reveals the prophet’s access to royal and military networks. The offer implies that the Shunammite woman might have legal or property disputes that required royal adjudication — a common anxiety for a wealthy woman in an honor-shame society where land tenure depended on male advocacy. Her reply ‘I dwell among my own people’ (bᵉtôḵ ʿammî ʾānōḵî yōšābet) is a declaration that her social situation is secure; she does not need political patronage.
4.14: Gehazi identifies the Shunammite’s barrenness and her husband’s old age — the double obstacle to an heir that placed her in a position of social vulnerability despite her current prosperity. Childlessness in the ancient Near East meant the eventual loss of property (no male heir to inherit), social marginalization in widowhood, and the extinction of the husband’s family name. The situation precisely mirrors the Abraham-Sarah narrative (Gen 18:10–14) and the Hannah narrative (1 Sam 1); barrenness resolved by prophetic word is a recurring structural pattern in Hebrew narrative.
4.15: Elisha calls the Shunammite and she ‘stood in the doorway’ (wattaʿămod bappetaḥ) — the threshold position that marks liminal encounters in Hebrew narrative. Standing at a doorway signals that the encounter is formal and momentous; the same posture appears at thresholds in other prophetic narratives. The shift from Gehazi as intermediary to Elisha addressing her directly signals that what follows transcends ordinary social exchange.
4.16: The Shunammite’s protest ‘do not deceive your servant’ (ʾal-tᵉkazzēb bᵉšipḥātekā) is not unbelief but appropriate caution: in the ANE world, raising and then dashing a barren woman’s hope of a child was a recognized cruelty. She is protecting herself from disappointment. The word for deceive (kāzab) is the opposite of prophetic truthfulness; she is, in effect, asking Elisha to confirm that this is a genuine prophetic word and not false consolation. The language echoes the Abrahamic annunciation where Sarah also expresses incredulity (Gen 18:12–15).
4.17: The fulfillment is dated to ‘about this time next year’ (kāʿēt ḥayyâ) — the same idiom used in Gen 18:10 and 14 for the Isaac annunciation. The verbal echo signals that the narrator is deliberately placing this account in the trajectory of the Abrahamic barrenness-and-birth pattern. The Shunammite becomes another figure in the series of women — Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah — whose barrenness is overcome by divine promise and whose sons carry forward significant roles in Israel’s story.
4.18: The child grows to the age when he can accompany his father to the harvest field — an economic unit of participation typical of agrarian societies. Children in ANE agricultural households participated in harvest work from an early age; the harvest scene establishes a normal, functioning family life before catastrophe strikes. The juxtaposition of the fulfilled promise (a growing child) with the sudden collapse (vv. 19–20) intensifies the narrative tension.
4.19: ‘My head! My head!’ (rōʾšî rōʾšî) — the doubled cry signals acute distress; in the harvest field context, heatstroke is the most plausible medical cause. Harvest work in the Jezreel Valley in summer involves intense sun exposure; sudden collapse from heat injury was a recognized danger. The father’s instruction to carry the child to his mother follows expected domestic protocol: medical crises within the household were managed in the women’s sphere.
4.20: The child dies at noon — the time of maximum heat in the agricultural day. The child’s death ‘on her lap’ establishes the most intimate possible setting; the Shunammite is positioned as both caregiver and the one who must now act. Her response — going up to the prophet’s room and laying the child on Elisha’s bed (v21) — is a deliberate prophetic claim: the place associated with divine presence is the appropriate location for a miracle she has not yet stopped expecting.
4.21: Placing the dead child on the prophet’s bed is a significant act — it appropriates the sacred space of the man of God for a petition that has not yet been spoken. The Shunammite does not mourn publicly or prepare the child for burial; she acts as one who believes the story is not finished. ANE parallels: the beds and sleeping chambers of holy men were treated as spaces of concentrated divine power; placing the dead there could be understood as making a ritual claim on that power.
4.22: The Shunammite’s request for a servant and donkey frames a journey of about 25–30 km to Carmel — not a quick trip. Her instruction to send ‘quickly’ (ûmāhartî wᵉhālaḵtî) establishes urgency without explaining why. She conceals her purpose from her husband, whose practical objection follows in v23. The donkey as transport animal is consistent with her elevated social standing; it was the standard vehicle for persons of means in the Israelite period.
4.23: The husband’s question ‘Why go to him today? It is neither a new moon nor a Sabbath’ reveals that pilgrimages to prophets typically occurred on festival days — new moon (ḥōdeš) and Sabbath (šabbāt) were the recognized times for consulting a man of God. This detail illuminates the calendar of prophetic accessibility in monarchic Israel: prophets were not always available; they received visitors on holy days. The Shunammite’s visit on an ordinary day marks the emergency she refuses to name.
4.24: The Shunammite’s instruction to the servant ‘do not slow down unless I tell you’ reflects the urgency of the situation. The word for ‘slow down’ (ʿāṣōr baʿădî rᵉḵōb’) concerns restraining the donkey’s pace; she is pressing for maximum speed within the limits of the animal. The overland route from Shunem to Mount Carmel crosses the Jezreel Valley — perhaps four to six hours of travel. Every element of her preparation is purposeful and urgent.
4.25: Mount Carmel had long associations with prophetic activity (cf. 1 Kgs 18, the Elijah-Baal contest) and apparently served as one of Elisha’s regular stations. The sight of the Shunammite ‘coming in the distance’ (seen from the high ground of Carmel) prompted Elisha’s prophetic discernment that something was wrong: ‘Her soul is bitter’ (wᵉnapšāh mārat lāh, v27). Prophetic intuition of spiritual distress is a standard feature of the prophetic narratives; Elisha perceives what Gehazi cannot.
4.26: Elisha sends Gehazi ahead with a standard welfare query: is it well with you (hăšālôm lāḵ)? The triple inquiry — ‘with you? with your husband? with the child?’ — follows the conventional pattern of village greeting that moved through the household unit. The Shunammite’s response ‘All is well (šālôm)’ is a deliberate suspension of disclosure — she is reserving her petition for Elisha himself, not his intermediary, even as she speaks the opposite of the truth about the child’s condition.
4.27: The Shunammite’s seizure of Elisha’s feet (wattaḥăzēq bᵉraglāyw) is the posture of urgent petition — prostration at the feet of a superior. Gehazi’s impulse to push her away reflects protocol norms; such contact with a holy man was considered inappropriate without invitation. Elisha’s counter-instruction ‘leave her alone’ overrides normal social restraint and signals that the prophetic encounter is to proceed without barriers. His acknowledgment that YHWH has ‘hidden it from me’ is a rare statement of prophetic limitation.
4.28: The Shunammite’s reproach ‘Did I ask my lord for a son?’ recalls her initial caution at v16. She had warned against false hope; now the fulfilled promise has been reversed. Her complaint is formally a qal waḥomer (a fortiori) argument: if I did not request this gift, how much more grievous is its loss? The theological tension — God gave, God (through the prophet) apparently took away — is not resolved here; it is the engine driving the subsequent miracle.
4.29: Elisha’s instruction to Gehazi to ‘tuck your garment into your belt’ (ḥăgōr motnêḵā) is the standard preparation for urgent travel in the ancient world — gathering up the long robe to free the legs for running. The prohibition on greeting anyone en route reflects an urgency that overrides normal social courtesy (cf. Luke 10:4, where Jesus uses similar language for the seventy). The staff as an extension of prophetic power (cf. Moses’ staff; Elisha’s own earlier use in 2:14) makes Gehazi’s mission a preliminary attempt at mediated restoration.
4.30: The Shunammite’s oath ‘As the LORD lives and as you yourself live’ (ḥay-yhwh wᵉḥay-napšᵉkā) is the strongest available oath formula in Hebrew; it invokes the life of God and the prophet’s own life as guarantors that she will not be dismissed with a proxy. Her refusal to let Elisha remain on Carmel without accompanying her is a demand for direct prophetic presence — not mediated power through a staff. Elisha complies; personal accompaniment becomes necessary for the miracle.
4.31: Gehazi’s staff laid on the boy’s face produces no response — ‘no sound and no sign of life’ (wᵉqôl wᵉʾên qešeb). The failure of the proximate, impersonal method establishes that the resurrection requires the prophet’s direct bodily presence. This structural pattern — failed preliminary attempt followed by successful personal intervention — appears also in 1 Kgs 17:21 (Elijah’s resurrection of the Zarephath widow’s son), indicating shared narrative convention for prophetic resurrection accounts in Kings.
4.32: The scene at the house — the child lying dead on the prophet’s own bed — sets up the confrontation with death in the private sacred space. Ancient Near Eastern background: the sleeping chambers of gods (in temple ideology) or holy men were associated with life-giving power; placing the dead child there was the Shunammite’s implicit claim on that power. The detail that the bed is specifically Elisha’s (miškāb hāʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm) is not incidental — it establishes the ritual claim.
4.33: Elisha’s prayer in the closed room mirrors Elijah’s pattern in 1 Kgs 17:20–21; prophetic resurrection involves direct address to YHWH in private. The closed door (as also in v4’s oil miracle) is a Sitz im Leben of prophetic concentration — withdrawal from the public sphere to address YHWH. The two-stage pattern of Elisha’s prayer (v33) followed by physical action (v34) reflects the view that resurrection power flows from YHWH in response to prophetic petition rather than from the prophet’s own power.
4.34: Elisha’s act of lying on the child — mouth to mouth, eyes to eyes, hands to hands (pîw ʿal-pîw wᵉʿênāyw ʿal-ʿênāyw wᵉkappāyw ʿal-kappāyw) — matches exactly Elijah’s action in 1 Kgs 17:21, establishing Elisha as Elijah’s true successor who performs the same acts of power. This physical positioning is not a primitive medical procedure (though the specificity is noted in medical history); it is a prophetic act of complete identification with the dead child, transmitting prophetic life-power through contact. The child’s flesh warming indicates the gradual return of life.
4.35: The two-stage miracle — Elisha rises, walks back and forth, then stretches over the child a second time — follows a pattern of prophetic persistence. The child’s sneezing seven times before opening his eyes is a detail of biological verisimilitude that has no ANE parallel. The number seven has conventional completeness in Hebrew idiom, but sneezing as a sign of returning life appears in some ancient texts (cf. the Egyptian story of Setne and the sneezing revived person). The physical detail anchors the miracle in observed bodily experience.
4.36: Elisha’s instruction ‘Take up your son’ (śᵉʾî binnēḵ) in the third person through Gehazi restores normal social protocol after the miracle — the Shunammite is now addressed as a mother receiving her child, not as a petitioner receiving a prophetic gift. The mother’s falling at Elisha’s feet (wattipōl ʿal-raglāyw) is gratitude in the same posture as petition (v27) — she who seized his feet in desperate appeal now bows before him in thanksgiving.
4.37: The Shunammite ‘went out’ with her son — the narrative closure returns her to the domestic space without further explanation. The economy of the ending is deliberate: the account is complete when the gift is restored; theological reflection is not supplied. The reader is expected to draw the connection between YHWH as the giver and restorer of life (v14–17 as promise; vv. 32–37 as fulfillment) and the prophetic role as mediator of that life-power.
4.38: Gilgal (probably the one near Jericho, one of Elisha’s circuit stops; cf. 2:1) during a famine — famines are periodically attested in the Levant through both biblical and extrabiblical sources. The ‘sons of the prophets sitting before him’ (yōšᵉbîm lᵉpānāyw) describes a teaching or consultative session in the prophetic community. Elisha’s instruction to cook a large pot (sir haggādōl) for the group reflects communal meal-sharing within the prophetic guild.
4.39: The ‘wild vine’ and the wild gourds gathered from it (paqquʿōt śādeh) are most commonly identified with the colocynth (Citrullus colocynthis), a desert plant producing attractive but intensely bitter and violently purgative fruit. In famine conditions, wild foods were gathered indiscriminately; the gatherer’s inexperience or desperation leads him to collect a harmful plant. The gourds sliced into the stew introduce a toxin that would produce acute gastric distress consistent with the men’s reaction.
4.40: ‘Death in the pot’ (māwet bassîr) — the men recognize the bitter, burning taste characteristic of colocynth poisoning. ANE herbal knowledge included recognition of toxic plants; the immediate identification and the refusal to eat further indicates practical culinary experience. The men’s appeal to Elisha as ‘man of God’ frames the situation as a crisis requiring prophetic intervention — they cannot consume their only available food.
4.41: Elisha’s use of flour (qemaḥ) to neutralize the toxin has generated discussion about its mode of action. The text does not present this as chemical neutralization but as a prophetic sign-act: the flour is the vehicle of the miracle, not its cause. Similar sign-acts where an unlikely material mediates divine power appear throughout Elisha’s ministry (salt purifying the spring in 2:21; a stick making iron float in 6:6). The point is that YHWH acts through prophetic command even using ordinary materials.
4.42: Baal-shalishah — a location in Ephraim (cf. 1 Sam 9:4), distinct from the great cult center Baal-Peor. The name’s connection to the Baalized toponymy of northern Israel is noted but does not affect the account. The offering of twenty barley loaves from firstfruits (leḥem bikkûrîm) is significant: firstfruits were consecrated to YHWH and typically brought to a sanctuary or a recognized representative of divine service. Bringing firstfruits to Elisha confirms his status as the legitimate locus of prophetic-priestly authority in the northern kingdom during this period.
4.43: The servant’s objection ‘How can I set this before a hundred men?’ establishes the scarcity: twenty loaves for a hundred people is a fifth of normal provision. Elisha’s response — ‘Give it to the people to eat, for thus says the LORD’ — invokes the prophetic formula (kōh ʾāmar yhwh) as the ground for an impossible instruction. The multiplication miracle with its ‘and they had some left over’ (cf. John 6:12–13) follows the same pattern as the widow’s oil: divine provision exceeds the apparent limit.
4.44: The closing ‘according to the word of the LORD’ (kidbar yhwh) attributes the multiplication explicitly to YHWH’s word spoken through Elisha. The Baal-shalishah feeding functions as a prophetic sign in the tradition of the wilderness manna: YHWH provides for his people through the prophetic word even in conditions of scarcity. The detail of ‘some left over’ echoes the wilderness surplus (Exod 16:17–18) and anticipates Gospel multiplication accounts where surplus remainder confirms the divine origin of the provision.

5.10: Elisha’s instruction to Naaman — hālôḵ wᵉrāḥaṣtā šeḇaʿ pᵉʿāmîm bayyardēn, ‘go and wash yourself seven times in the Jordan’ — is medically, ritually, and geographically specific. The ṣāraʿat (conventionally translated ‘leprosy’ but covering a range of skin conditions in the Levitical purity system, Lev 13-14) was the purity condition that required a seven-step ritual cleansing in the Levitical law (Lev 14:1-9: the cleansed person washes clothes seven days). The number seven in the Elisha instruction echoes Levitical ritual completeness. The Jordan’s choice is geographically pointed: Naaman wants the rivers of Damascus, which are ‘better’ by any measure of water quality (v12: Abana and Pharpar). The Jordan is the covenant-boundary water of Israelite religious geography — the water Joshua crossed (Josh 3), the water John will baptize in, the water at which Elijah and Elisha parted the waters (2 Kgs 2:8, 14). The healing occurs in the covenant-water of YHWH’s people, not in the superior rivers of Damascus — signaling that cleanness is defined by YHWH’s covenant, not by natural quality. The seven-fold immersion in covenant-water is the ritual precursor for the NT’s baptismal theology: entry into cleanness through the water of covenant-belonging (Rom 6:4).
5.17: Naaman’s request for ‘two mule-loads of earth (ʾădāmāh)’ to take back to Aram is a window into standard ANE territorial-deity theology. Throughout the ancient Near East, deities were understood as geographically bound: the gods of a nation were associated with its land, temples, and territory; a worshipper of a particular god who relocated to foreign soil required either the worship of the new territory’s gods or some way of bringing the deity’s territory with them. The Assyrian inscriptions and Egyptian wisdom texts document the assumption that divine power was territorially limited. Naaman’s request is coherent within this framework: to worship YHWH in Damascus requires Israelite soil as the physical substrate of YHWH’s presence. Elisha’s response — lēḵ lᵉšālôm, ‘go in peace’ — neither endorses the theology nor corrects it explicitly; the narrator leaves the theological irony implicit. The prior verse (v15: ‘now I know that there is no God in all the earth but in Israel’) is a more advanced theology than the two-mule-loads request that follows, suggesting Naaman’s understanding is transitional. Isa 40:12-31 provides the theological correction: YHWH is not territorially limited but measures the waters in the hollow of his hand; he is the creator of the ends of the earth. John 4:21-24 (Jesus to the Samaritan woman: ‘the hour is coming when neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem will you worship the Father’) is the final dissolution of the territorial-worship assumption that the two-mule-loads request embodies.
5.1: Naaman (naʿămān, ‘pleasantness’) is the commander of the Aramean army — śar-ṣᵉbāʾ melek ʾărām — at a time when Aram-Damascus was the dominant power in the southern Levant. The text’s note that ‘through him the LORD had given victory to Aram’ (bô nātan-yhwh tᵉšûʿâ lᵃʾărām) is theologically audacious: YHWH is credited with military victories for a foreign nation, suggesting that YHWH’s sovereignty extends to international politics, not merely Israelite history. His tsara’at — traditionally translated leprosy but more broadly a skin condition causing ritual impurity — would have been a severe disability in a military and court context, affecting his social role without necessarily incapacitating him physically (he is still active as commander).
5.2: Aramean raiding parties (gᵉdûdîm) into Israel took captives as labor and tribute. The ‘little girl’ (naʿărāh qᵉṭannāh) who serves Naaman’s wife has been separated from her family and homeland by slave raid — a routine feature of Iron Age warfare in the Levant, documented in Assyrian records as well as biblical narrative. Her ethnic identity as Israelite is significant: the knowledge of YHWH’s prophet in Samaria exists at the margins of Aramean power, carried by a nameless enslaved child. The narrative’s structure places the reversal of Israel’s powerlessness in the mouth of its most vulnerable member.
5.3: The girl’s declaration — ‘if only my lord were before the prophet in Samaria’ — is expressed as a wish (lû + jussive), reflecting her limited authority to initiate action. Her confident assertion that the prophet ‘would cure him of his leprosy’ (wᵉʾāsap ʾōtô mitstsāraʿtô) frames prophetic healing as known fact, not speculation. A slave’s testimony in an aristocratic household would normally carry little weight, but the urgency of Naaman’s condition elevates any potential solution. The transmission of prophetic reputation across ethnic and political boundaries — from an Israelite slave to an Aramean general — illustrates how YHWH’s name circulates in the international sphere.
5.4: Naaman’s report to the king of Aram treats the slave girl’s testimony as actionable intelligence. The chain of authority — slave girl → Naaman’s wife → Naaman → king of Aram — illustrates how information flowed through hierarchical ANE court structures. That the word of a foreign Israelite slave girl reaches the king of Aram and initiates a diplomatic mission indicates the credibility that Elisha’s reputation had achieved, even in enemy territory.
5.5: The gift Naaman brings for his healing is enormous by any measure: ten talents of silver (approximately 340 kg or 750 lb), six thousand shekels of gold (approximately 70 kg or 150 lb), and ten changes of clothing — a multi-year national budget for a small city-state. This level of payment reflects both the value placed on healing from tsara’at (which carried social and cultic exclusion) and the ANE convention that supernatural services — including the services of diviners, dream interpreters, and healers — commanded proportional payment. The king of Aram frames the mission as a diplomatic visit with a commercial dimension, not as a religious consultation.
5.6: The letter from the king of Aram to the king of Israel treats the healing transaction as a royal matter: king to king. The phrasing ‘I have sent you my servant Naaman that you may cure him’ creates a diplomatic trap: if the king of Israel cannot or will not comply, it becomes a casus belli. ANE royal correspondence (extensively documented in the Amarna letters from the 14th century BCE) frequently deployed this form — using diplomatic language to create obligations that could not easily be refused without offense.
5.7: The king of Israel’s tearing of garments (wayyiqraʿ ʾet-bᵉgādāyw) is the standard mourning-and-distress gesture in the Hebrew Bible, here used to express political panic rather than grief. His rhetorical question — ‘Am I God, able to kill and to make alive?’ (hăʾĕlōhîm ʾānî lᵉmāmît ûlᵉhaḥăyôt) — inadvertently makes the correct theological point: healing leprosy is a divine prerogative. His suspicion that the request is a pretext for war reflects the volatile political context of the Aramean-Israelite relationship (cf. 1 Kgs 20; 22). He does not think of Elisha, who is the actual locus of divine power.
5.8: Elisha’s message to the king — ‘Let him come to me, and he shall know that there is a prophet in Israel’ (wᵉyēdaʿ kî yēš nābîʾ bᵉyiśrāʾēl) — reframes the encounter from a political to a prophetic event. The phrase ‘there is a prophet in Israel’ asserts that Israel’s real resource in this situation is not royal diplomacy but the presence of a man of God. Elisha inserts himself into the diplomatic chain and takes responsibility for the outcome — a bold prophetic claim in the face of an international crisis.
5.9: Naaman arrives at Elisha’s house with full military retinue — horses and chariots (bᵉsûsāyw ûbᵉriḵbô) — displaying his rank and the Aramean king’s investment in the mission. The scene is staged for maximum contrast: Aramean military power at the door of an Israelite prophet’s house. Elisha does not come out to meet him — a deliberate protocol reversal. In ANE royal and elite culture, the greater power would be received; Elisha’s non-appearance signals that the expected social hierarchies are suspended.
5.11: Naaman’s anger reflects the violation of honor-shame expectations: he anticipated a formal healing ritual — the prophet would ‘come out to me in person, stand and call on the name of the LORD his God, and wave his hand over the spot’ (yāṣōʾ yāṣōʾ wᵉʿāmad wᵉqārāʾ bᵉšēm yhwh ʾĕlōhāyw’). This expected gesture — hand-waving over the afflicted area — is attested in ANE magical healing texts from Mesopotamia and Egypt. Naaman expected a performance appropriate to his status as a great man; the messenger’s instruction violated both the expected ritual form and the expected social deference.
5.12: Naaman’s mention of the Abana and Pharpar rivers (the main rivers of Damascus, modern Barada and Nahr al-Awaj) as superior to ‘all the waters of Israel’ is not simply nationalistic pride but reflects genuine hydrological superiority: the rivers of the Damascus oasis were perennially flowing, cold mountain streams, while the Jordan was a slower, lowland river, murkier in places. From a pragmatic standpoint, Naaman’s assessment is reasonable. His rejection of the Jordan is also a rejection of what the Jordan represents — the distinctive body of water associated with Israel’s crossing into the land and Elijah’s crossing in 2:8.
5.13: Naaman’s servants address him with the honorific ‘my father’ (ʾābî) — a term of respectful address that could be used by subordinates for a senior authority figure. Their logic is a qal waḥomer: if you would do something hard for the prophet, how much more something easy? The question ‘how much more, then, when he says to you, Wash and be clean?’ reverses his expectation — the very ease of the instruction should recommend it. The servants’ counsel prevails over Naaman’s pride; in the narrative structure, it is the subordinate voices (the slave girl in v3; the servants here) who carry the true prophetic message.
5.14: The Jordan dipping seven times — a number of completeness in Hebrew idiom — is specifically prescribed by the prophetic word; deviating from the number (stopping at five, say) would presumably not have achieved the result. Naaman’s restoration — ‘his flesh was restored like the flesh of a young child’ (wayyāšob bᵉśārô kᵉbᵉśar naʿar qāṭōn) — uses language of reversal: what was corrupted and aging is returned to a state of youth and wholeness. This is not merely medical cure but social and cultic rehabilitation: healed from tsara’at meant re-entry into community, worship, and normal social function.
5.15: Naaman’s confession — ‘I know that there is no God in all the earth except in Israel’ (hinnēh-nāʾ yādaʿtî kî ʾên ʾĕlōhîm bᵉḵol-hāʾāreṣ kî ʾim-bᵉyiśrāʾēl) — is a monotheistic declaration by a foreigner, comparable to Rahab’s confession (Josh 2:11) and the queen of Sheba’s acknowledgment (1 Kgs 10:9). The returned ‘gift’ (bᵉrāḵāh, blessing) offered to Elisha indicates that Naaman now understands the healing as YHWH’s act through a prophet, not a commercial transaction. The pattern — Gentile healed, Gentile confesses YHWH — is later cited by Jesus as a sign of YHWH’s universal sovereignty (Luke 4:27).
5.16: Elisha’s refusal of payment — sworn by the oath ‘As the LORD lives, before whom I stand’ (ḥay-yhwh ʾăšer-ʿāmadtî lᵉpānāyw) — distinguishes prophetic service from the commercial healing that characterized professional diviners and physicians in the ANE world. Prophets in Israel were sometimes paid (1 Sam 9:7–8; cf. Balaam), but Elisha here refuses on principle: accepting payment from Naaman would suggest that the healing was a commercial service rather than a sign of YHWH’s sovereign grace operating through the prophet. The refusal also prevents any equation of YHWH’s power with marketable Aramean goods.
5.18: Naaman’s request for prophetic tolerance of his continued attendance in the temple of Rimmon addresses a real dilemma for a foreign monotheist in an official position. Rimmon is the storm-deity Hadad (the name ‘Rimmon’ or Rammān means ‘Thunderer’), the principal deity of Aram-Damascus, analogous to Baal in Canaan and Adad in Mesopotamia. As commander and presumably a royal official, Naaman was expected to participate in state religious ceremonies. His request — ‘may the LORD pardon your servant in this matter’ — is not cynical compromise but a sincere anticipation of moral conflict and a request for prophetic ruling on the permissibility of his situation.
5.19: Elisha’s response ‘Go in peace’ (lēḵ lᵉšālôm) is a standard farewell formula but carries significant weight here: it is not a rebuke, a prohibition, or a condition. The text does not amplify with any ruling on the Rimmon question; Elisha simply dismisses Naaman in peace. This ambiguity has generated extensive discussion: some interpreters see it as tacit tolerance of accommodation under constraint; others as a simple farewell without implied endorsement. The narrative leaves the question open, perhaps deliberately. Naaman departs as a confessing monotheist navigating a complex diplomatic role — a liminal figure the text does not condemn.
5.20: Gehazi’s interior monologue reveals the commercial logic he applies to the encounter: his master has let Naaman ‘off without accepting what he brought’ — the massive gift of silver, gold, and clothing represents an enormous windfall foregone. The phrase ‘as the LORD lives, I will run after him and accept something from him’ parodies Elisha’s oath formula in v16 — Gehazi invokes YHWH’s name in support of the very commercial transaction Elisha refused in YHWH’s name. The irony is sharp: Gehazi claims YHWH’s authority for greed.
5.21: Naaman sees Gehazi running after him and steps down from his chariot to meet him — the gesture of social consideration toward someone of lower status signals Naaman’s genuine transformation. His question ‘Is everything all right?’ (hăšālôm) echoes the welfare formula of v26 and establishes the ironic contrast: Naaman, newly cleansed and confessing, extends considerate inquiry; Gehazi, standing in the prophetic household, is about to commit fraud.
5.22: Gehazi’s fabricated story — two young prophets have arrived and Elisha needs silver and clothing for them — invents a plausible prophetic-community need to justify the request. The ‘company of the prophets from the hill country of Ephraim’ is a geographically plausible reference; prophetic guilds were known in that region. Gehazi requests a fraction of what Naaman offered (one talent of silver vs. ten), making the deception seem modest and therefore credible. The request for clothing is particularly apt, since Naaman’s gift had specifically included it.
5.23: Naaman’s generosity in insisting on giving two talents rather than the one requested reflects his gratitude and the enormous surplus of what he brought. Two talents of silver is still a staggering sum — equivalent to perhaps 70 kg of silver — far more than needed for any plausible prophetic community need. Naaman ties the silver in two bags and assigns two of his own servants to carry them back with Gehazi — a courtesy to the assumed prophets of Israel, and an inadvertent witness to Gehazi’s deception.
5.24: ‘The hill’ (hāʿōpel) refers to a specific elevated part of the approach to the city — possibly the Ophel, though the northern Israel context makes a different location more likely. Gehazi takes the bags from Naaman’s servants, deposits them inside, and sends the servants back — all before presenting himself to Elisha. The concealment and the sequential actions indicate premeditation, not impulsive theft. Gehazi is methodically managing the cover of his deception.
5.25: Elisha’s question ‘Where have you been, Gehazi?’ and Gehazi’s denial ‘Your servant hasn’t been anywhere’ sets up the prophetic exposure that follows. The denial uses a strong Hebrew idiom of absence: lōʾ-hālaḵ ʿabdᵉḵā ʾānnâ wᵉʾānnâ, ‘your servant did not go anywhere at all.’ The redundancy intensifies the lie. Elisha’s knowledge of Gehazi’s movements — ‘did not my heart go with you?’ (v26) — reflects the prophetic omniscience that makes deception futile; the prophet sees what the servant thinks is hidden.
5.26: ‘Is this the time to accept silver, clothing, olive groves, vineyards, sheep, oxen, and servants?’ (hāʿēt lāqaḥat et-hakkesep wᵉlāqaḥat bᵉgādîm) — Elisha’s list expands far beyond what Gehazi actually received, describing the full range of wealth accumulation. The prophetic critique targets not just this specific act but the mercenary orientation it represents. The ‘time’ (ʿēt) argument draws on prophetic calendrical consciousness: this was the moment when Naaman had been healed and confessed YHWH; commodifying that moment by accepting payment corrupts the witness value of the healing.
5.27: The transfer of Naaman’s leprosy to Gehazi and his descendants is the judicial sentence that closes the account. ‘Cling to you and your descendants forever’ (tidbaq-bᵉḵā ûbᵉzarʿᵃḵā lᵉʿôlām) — the same condition Naaman was healed of becomes the permanent mark of the servant’s betrayal. The irony is structural: Gehazi traded the healing miracle for silver, and the miracle is reversed against him. Naaman goes away white-skinned no more (wᵉhûʾ mᵉṣōrāʿ kaššāleg); Gehazi goes away ‘white as snow’ — carrying what the Aramean commander left behind.

6.17: Elisha’s prayer that his servant’s eyes be opened to see the mountain full of horses and chariots of fire (sûsîm wᵉreḵeḇ ʾēš sāḇîḇ lᵉʾelîšāʿ) deploys the ANE’s most powerful divine-warrior imagery. The celestial chariotry motif is widespread in ancient Near Eastern divine-warrior texts: Egyptian hymns to Amun-Re depict the god riding his solar chariot across the heavens; Akkadian mythology includes divine chariots in celestial combat. In Israelite tradition the reḵeḇ ʾelōhîm (chariots of God) appears in Ps 68:17 (twenty thousand chariots, thousands upon thousands) and the divine-warrior tradition. Elijah’s departure was in a chariot of fire (2 Kgs 2:11-12), and Elisha called him ‘the chariots of Israel and its horsemen’ at the departure (reḵeḇ yiśrāʾēl ûpārāšāyw, 2 Kgs 2:12; 13:14 — the same cry used for Elisha at his death). The theological claim is precise: the true military strength of Israel is not its standing army but YHWH’s invisible celestial forces — more numerous and more powerful than the Aramean host. The Elisha narrative provides the narrative grounding for Ps 46:11 (‘the Lord of hosts is with us; the God of Jacob is our fortress’): what Elisha’s servant sees in vision is the ongoing reality behind the psalm’s confidence.
6.25: The besieged Samaria’s famine conditions — a donkey’s head sold for eighty shekels of silver and dove’s dung (or possibly carob pods) for five shekels — are siege economics at their extreme. The catastrophic siege-famine is the enacted form of the covenant curse of Lev 26:29 (‘you shall eat the flesh of your sons’) and Deut 28:53-57, both of which predict cannibalism as the extreme expression of covenant judgment under siege: ‘you will eat the fruit of your womb, the flesh of your sons and daughters, whom YHWH your God has given you.’ The cannibalism reported to the king in vv26-29 (one woman eating her son in accordance with a pact with another woman who has hidden hers) is the literal fulfillment of Deut 28:53-57 and Lev 26:29. The Deuteronomic structure is the author’s interpretive frame: the siege of Samaria is not simply military misfortune but covenant curse enacted. Lam 4:10 applies the same image to the Babylonian siege of Jerusalem: ‘the hands of compassionate women have boiled their own children; they became their food during the destruction of my people.’ The besieged city eating its children is the OT’s most extreme covenant-curse image.
6.1: The sons of the prophets (b’nê hannebî’îm) describe their living space as ‘too cramped’ (tsār mimmennû) — a growing community around Elisha whose housing needs have exceeded the current facility. This casual detail illuminates the prophetic-guild structure: these were residential communities, not solitary ascetics. The setting near the Jordan (v2) and the presumably modest accommodation suggest a community of intentional simplicity that is nonetheless a genuine social institution with practical needs.
6.2: The proposal to go to the Jordan and ‘each get a log’ (wᵉniqqaḥ miššām ʾîš qôrâ ʾeḥāt) for building reflects the availability of timber near the Jordan riparian zone. The Jordan Valley in this period had denser vegetation than today; riverside timber was accessible, though large stands of forest were already depleted in much of Canaan. The communal self-help building project — each member responsible for one log — reflects the egalitarian labor-sharing of prophetic guild community life.
6.3: The request that Elisha accompany his servants — ‘Please come with your servants’ — and his compliance are noted as an act of prophetic solidarity. Elisha participates in the physical labor and relocation of his community rather than remaining at a distance as a purely spiritual authority. His willingness to go with them places him in proximity to the subsequent crisis (the lost axe head) where his intervention is needed.
6.4: The narrative economy of the journey to the Jordan is typical of prophetic-cycle accounts: the minimum detail necessary to establish the setting. ‘They began cutting down trees’ (wᵉyiggᵉzᵉrû hāʿēṣîm) — the work begins immediately upon arrival. The communal nature of the work is implied by the plural subject; the individual whose axe head falls (v5) is one member of this collective project.
6.5: The axe head falling into the water was a genuine crisis in the Iron Age economy. Iron tools were expensive relative to wages — an iron axe head could represent several months of a laborer’s income. The man’s anguished cry ‘Oh, my master! It was borrowed’ (ʾăhāh ʾădonî wᵉhûʾ šāʾûl) introduces the economic and social dimension: he is liable for the loss of a borrowed tool, an obligation he cannot meet from his resources as a member of the prophetic guild. The debt-slavery backdrop of ch4:1 is relevant here: an unpayable debt creates a serious social problem.
6.6: Elisha’s action — cutting a stick (wayyiqṣōb ʿēṣ) and throwing it in at the spot — is a sign-act, not a technical solution. Iron does not float under any circumstances; the miracle is the suspended law of density. The stick thrown in may echo the Mosaic pattern of staff-actions (Exod 14:16; 15:25, where Moses throws a stick into bitter water to make it sweet), suggesting Elisha’s continuing ministry in the prophetic line from Moses. The act of locating the exact spot first grounds the miracle in the physical specificity of the real event.
6.7: ‘Pick it up’ (rōmm-lāḵ) — the floating iron is not seized by Elisha but retrieved by the man himself, using his own hand. The miracle does not bypass human agency but enables it. The man reaches out and takes what was lost — the restoration of the tool is the restoration of his social obligation. The brevity of the miracle’s resolution (two words in Hebrew) matches its function as a brief sign within a longer narrative section about Aramean warfare.
6.8: The Aramean king’s war strategy depended on ambush and surprise encampment (tiḥneh maḥăneh) — a standard ancient warfare tactic of concealing troop positions before an engagement. The phrase ‘he would consult with his officers’ (wayyiwwāʿaṣ ʾel-ʿăbādāyw) describes the formal council of war that preceded tactical decisions in ANE armies. The Israelite king’s uncanny awareness of Aramean plans sets the stage for the prophetic explanation in vv. 11–12.
6.9: Elisha sends warning to the king of Israel: ‘Beware of passing that place — the Arameans are going down there.’ The prophetic role as military intelligence is extraordinary: Elisha functions as a one-man signals corps, rendering the Aramean strategic advantage — surprise positioning — ineffective. The phrase ‘he warned him’ (wayyizzāhēr’) uses a root associated with prophetic warning; the intelligence is presented not as espionage but as divinely communicated knowledge.
6.10: ‘This happened not once or twice’ (welōʾ-ʾaḥat wᵉlōʾ-šetayim) — the phrase signals repeated occurrences, establishing a pattern before the Aramean king recognizes that there is a systematic intelligence leak. The king of Israel’s repeated successful avoidances of Aramean positions is not attributed to his own intelligence network but to the prophetic word — a consistent theme in the Elisha cycle that repositions political and military power relative to prophetic authority.
6.11: The Aramean king’s accusation that ‘one of us’ (mēʾittānû) is reporting to Israel reflects the logic of counter-intelligence: repeated strategic failure must indicate betrayal. This was a genuine anxiety in ANE court culture, where palace intrigue, divided loyalties, and diplomatic double-dealing were common. The accusation implicitly assumes that the intelligence is human rather than divine — the king cannot conceive of a supernatural source for Israel’s foreknowledge.
6.12: The officer’s explanation — ‘Elisha the prophet who is in Israel tells the king of Israel the very words you speak in your bedroom’ (yagîd lᵉmelek yiśrāʾēl ʾet-haddᵉbārîm ʾăšer tᵉdabbēr bᵉḥeder miškābekā) — credits prophetic power with a penetration of the innermost privacy of the royal chamber. The bedchamber (ḥeder miškab) is the most private space in the palace; the implication is that no human intelligence could reach there. The Aramean officer’s attribution of Elisha’s knowledge to supernatural access is the correct explanation — but it makes the prophet a threat to be neutralized, not a resource to be consulted.
6.13: Dothan (modern Tell Dothan) is located about 20 km north of Samaria, near a pass in the Samarian hills that served as a major route of ancient travel and trade (cf. Gen 37:17, where Joseph finds his brothers there). The city controlled access to the Jezreel Valley from the south; its location made it a logical point of Aramean military interest. Elisha’s presence in Dothan is incidental to the main narrative — he is simply there when the crisis arrives, suggesting that his movements were known and tracked by Aramean intelligence.
6.14: The Aramean response is disproportionate — horses, chariots, and ‘a great army’ (ḥayil kābēd) sent to capture a single prophet. The tactical choice of a night arrival and surrounding the city reflects standard siege and capture protocol: arrive under cover of darkness, prevent escape, then seize the target at first light. The military force sent against Elisha is the measure of his perceived threat to Aramean strategic planning; from the Aramean perspective, eliminating the intelligence source is a strategic priority.
6.15: The servant’s early morning discovery — ‘an army with horses and chariots had surrounded the city’ — produces the expected response: ‘Oh, my master! What shall we do?’ (ʾăhāh ʾădonî māh naʿăśeh). The exclamation ʾăhāh is the same as the axe-head man’s cry in v5 — a marker of acute distress at a humanly irresolvable situation. The servant’s question frames the crisis as a question of available resources: none visible, against an overwhelming military force. Elisha’s response reframes the question in terms of invisible reality.
6.16: ‘Those who are with us are more than those who are with them’ (rabbîm ʾăšer ʾittānû miʾăšer ʾittām) is the interpretive key to the following vision. The statement precedes the opened eyes: it is a claim about existing reality, not a reassurance about what will happen. In ANE cosmology, the heavenly armies (celestial or angelic beings associated with YHWH; cf. Josh 5:14, the ‘commander of the LORD’s army’) were understood as real participants in earthly conflicts. Elisha’s claim is not rhetorical comfort; it is a statement of military fact that the servant’s normal vision cannot confirm.
6.18: Elisha’s prayer for blindness (siwwᵉrôn) against the descending Aramean army is not the physical blindness that prevents all light perception but a confusion that prevents accurate spatial recognition — they can still travel and follow Elisha but cannot identify where they are. Similar ‘blinding’ or disorientation of attacking enemies appears in Gen 19:11 (the Sodom mob struck blind by the angels). The miracle is proportionate: the Aramean army is not destroyed but redirected — led into a trap that becomes a feast (v23).
